title: Reflection-Positive Construction of a Four-Dimensional SU(N) Yang-Mills Theory with Mass Gap and Confinement
comment: The detailed calculations supporting the results of the present paper have been published as a series of four papers: Int. J. Geom. Meth. Mod. Phys. (2026) 2650114, Int. J. Geom. Meth. Mod. Phys. (2026) 2650113, Int. J. Geom. Meth. Mod. Phys. (2026) 2650112, Int. J. Geom. Meth. Mod. Phys. (2026) 2650111. For the reader's convenience, these papers are included at the end of the present paper
summary: In the Euclidean view one must first require that positivity not be violated, and from this modest demand, together with locality, a great deal follows: starting from a reflection-positive lattice formulation of pure SU(N) Yang-Mills theory we obtain a transfer operator with a uniform gap, while large Wilson loops already show an area law by means of convergent character (polymer) expansions; a finite-range, gauge-covariant multiscale analysis then carries these features from one scale to the next with interlaced inequalities whose small defects can be summed, so that exponential clustering and a strictly positive string tension endure in the continuum; the Osterwalder-Schrader reconstruction turns these Euclidean facts into a Minkowski theory with a self-adjoint Hamiltonian, the spectral gap lying above the vacuum and the linear potential for static charges appearing, which gives a concrete picture of confinement; the construction depends on no special regulator, for a single-scale Lipschitz control and a telescoping argument bind all admissible reflection-positive slicings into a unique limiting measure and thus secure universality; moreover, the same framework admits entry from weak coupling, so that the continuum reached from strong coupling meets the one approached along an asymptotically free trajectory, yielding one and the same theory; in my view this is how mathematical clarity and physical insight cooperate: positivity, locality, and renormalization working together so that the mass gap and confinement are not marvels to be assumed, but natural properties of the non-Abelian vacuum.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses a method for establishing the mass gap and confinement in pure SU(N) Yang-Mills theory using positivity, locality, and renormalization, leading to a unique limiting measure that is independent of the regulator and connects strong and weak coupling regimes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a mathematically rigorous framework that explains the physical phenomena of mass gap and confinement as natural outcomes of non-Abelian gauge theories, rather than as assumptions. The work aims to show how these properties emerge from fundamental principles like positivity and locality, through a multiscale analysis and Osterwalder-Schrader reconstruction, thus bridging the gap between Euclidean and Minkowski space descriptions.
AI.method: The method involves constructing a reflection-positive lattice formulation of pure SU(N) Yang-Mills theory, which leads to a transfer operator with a uniform gap and large Wilson loops obeying an area law. A finite-range, gauge-covariant multiscale analysis is then used to propagate these features across scales, ensuring exponential clustering and positive string tension in the continuum limit. The Osterwalder-Schrader axioms are applied to reconstruct the Minkowski space theory, yielding a self-adjoint Hamiltonian with a spectral gap above the vacuum and a linear potential for static charges, indicating confinement. Universality is ensured by demonstrating that the results are independent of the specific slicing or regulator used, thanks to a single-scale Lipschitz control and a telescoping argument.
AI.result: The result is a construction that shows the emergence of a mass gap and confinement in the continuum limit of pure SU(N) Yang-Mills theory, which is consistent with both strong and weak coupling regimes. This provides a coherent picture where the mass gap and confinement arise naturally from the underlying principles of positivity, locality, and renormalization, without requiring additional assumptions. The uniqueness of the limiting measure suggests that the theory is universal, meaning it does not depend on the choice of regulator.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion drawn is that the mathematical framework developed in this work, which combines positivity, locality, and renormalization, successfully explains the physical phenomena of mass gap and confinement in non-Abelian gauge theories. This approach offers a clear and physically insightful way to understand these phenomena as intrinsic properties of the non-Abelian vacuum, rather than as ad hoc assumptions, and it demonstrates the power of combining mathematical rigor with physical intuition.

title: Kernel transformations and bounds for smeared spectral functions
summary: This work develops a framework for transforming between smeared spectral functions computed using different smearing kernels. The kernel-transformation problem naturally arises when information is available for one family of energy-smeared observables, while phenomenology or comparison with other calculations require a different smearing. For exact transformations, analytic conditions are established for the maps to exist and converge without arbitrary regularization. Explicit expressions are provided for several kernel classes of interest, including Cauchy-to-Gaussian transformations and Gaussian-to-Cauchy width mixtures. When exact transformations are unavailable, the inverse problem is tackled through regulated maps paired with bounds on the associated systematic error, directly computable from the given input data. Errors on the input smeared spectral functions, either statistical or in the form of pointwise rigorous bounds, are then propagated to the target observables. Enforcing spectral positivity can be used to tighten the bounds.

AI.tldr: A framework for transforming smeared spectral functions between different smearing kernels is developed, providing exact and approximate transformation methods, with error propagation and bounds, to enable accurate comparisons and phenomenological applications.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is the need to transform information from one family of energy-smeared observables to another, as required by different calculations or for phenomenological studies. This is necessary when the available data does not match the specific form needed for comparison or further analysis.
AI.method: The authors establish analytic conditions for the existence and convergence of transformation maps without arbitrary regularization. They provide explicit expressions for several kernel classes, including Cauchy-to-Gaussian and Gaussian-to-Cauchy width mixtures. For cases where exact transformations are not feasible, they introduce regulated maps and calculate associated systematic errors, which are directly computable from the input data. Additionally, they propagate errors on the input functions, whether statistical or in the form of rigorous bounds, to the target observables, and use spectral positivity to tighten these bounds.
AI.result: The paper presents a comprehensive approach to transforming smeared spectral functions, offering both exact and approximate methods. It provides tools for calculating systematic errors and propagating uncertainties, ensuring that the transformed functions can be used reliably in further analyses or comparisons. The use of spectral positivity is shown to improve the accuracy of the bounds on the transformed functions.
AI.conclusion: The work contributes a valuable framework for the transformation of smeared spectral functions, facilitating more accurate and versatile use of experimental and theoretical data in various physical contexts. By addressing the challenges of error propagation and transformation accuracy, it enhances the applicability of spectral function data across different smearing schemes.

title: Scalar diquark mass and quark--diquark potential from lattice QCD using the potential method with a static quark
summary: We study the scalar diquark mass and the quark--diquark potential by applying a HAL QCD-inspired potential method to a baryonic system composed of a scalar diquark and a static quark. The diquark mass is determined self-consistently by requiring that the p-wave baryonic spectrum obtained from two-point correlators be reproduced within the potential framework. Numerical calculations are performed using $2+1$ flavor QCD gauge configurations generated by the PACS-CS Collaboration on a $L^{3} \times T = 32^{3} \times 64$ lattice with $a^{-1} \approx 2.176$ GeV and the pion mass, $m_π \approx 702$ MeV. From the analysis, we obtain a scalar diquark mass which is close to the naïve constituent quark estimate $ (2/3)m_{N}$, together with a quark--diquark potential of the Cornell type (Coulomb + linear). The string tension extracted from the quark--diquark potential agrees within approximately 5% with that obtained from the static quark--antiquark potential (Wilson Loop).

AI.tldr: The study uses a HAL QCD-inspired potential method to determine the scalar diquark mass and quark-diquark potential in a baryonic system, finding a scalar diquark mass close to (2/3)m_N and a Cornell-type potential with a string tension agreeing within 5% of that from the static quark-antiquark potential.
AI.motivation: To understand the properties of baryons, specifically the scalar diquark mass and the quark-diquark interaction potential, by applying a method inspired by the HAL QCD approach to lattice QCD data.
AI.method: A HAL QCD-inspired potential method is applied to a system of a scalar diquark and a static quark using 2+1 flavor QCD gauge configurations on a 32^3 x 64 lattice with a pion mass of about 702 MeV. The scalar diquark mass is determined self-consistently by matching the p-wave baryonic spectrum from two-point correlators with the one obtained from the potential framework.
AI.result: The scalar diquark mass is found to be close to the naïve constituent quark estimate (2/3)m_N, and the quark-diquark potential exhibits a Cornell-type form (Coulomb + linear). The extracted string tension is consistent within 5% with that from the static quark-antiquark potential analysis (Wilson Loop).
AI.conclusion: The HAL QCD-inspired potential method successfully describes the quark-diquark system, providing a scalar diquark mass consistent with expectations and a potential form that matches the well-known Cornell potential, with a string tension that agrees with the static quark-antiquark case.

title: A Finite-Volume Scheme for the Continuum Extrapolation of Lattice Step-Scaling in (2+1)D Hamiltonian U(1) Gauge Theory
summary: We propose a finite-volume scheme to perform controlled continuum extrapolations of the lattice step-scaling function, a key ingredient for determining the running coupling in a Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory in small volumes. As a testbed, we employ a dual Hamiltonian formulation of pure U(1) gauge theory in (2+1) dimensions and an operator basis that remains efficient toward weak coupling. We describe the implementation of static external charges on the spatial lattice and study, using matrix product states, the resulting confining string, from which we extract the static potential and a force-based renormalized coupling. Using the proposed finite-volume scheme, we demonstrate a stable continuum limit of the step-scaling function on the lattice sizes accessible to present Hamiltonian simulations. The method is readily extendable to other gauge groups and dimensions, providing a pathway toward Hamiltonian step-scaling studies in other theories.

AI.tldr: A finite-volume scheme is proposed for controlled continuum extrapolations of the lattice step-scaling function in a Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory, demonstrated with a (2+1)D U(1) gauge theory, showing a stable continuum limit and potential application to other theories.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a method that allows for accurate determination of the running coupling in small volumes within a Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory, which can be extended to other gauge groups and dimensions.
AI.method: The authors use a dual Hamiltonian formulation of pure U(1) gauge theory in (2+1) dimensions, implement static external charges on the spatial lattice, and study the confining string using matrix product states to extract the static potential and a force-based renormalized coupling. They employ a finite-volume scheme to perform controlled continuum extrapolations of the lattice step-scaling function.
AI.result: The result is a demonstration of a stable continuum limit of the step-scaling function on lattice sizes that are accessible to current Hamiltonian simulations, indicating the effectiveness of the proposed finite-volume scheme.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed finite-volume scheme successfully achieves a stable continuum limit of the step-scaling function and provides a pathway for Hamiltonian step-scaling studies in other theories, including those with different gauge groups and in various dimensions.

title: Hamiltonian-based dimensional reduction and spectral reconstruction with Wilson-Dirac fermions
comment: Contribution to the proceedings of the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), Mumbai, India
summary: Motivated by the process of reconstructing real-time spectral functions from Euclidean correlators in lattice QCD, we derive explicit expressions for the fermionic determinant and the propagator of the four-dimensional clover-improved Wilson-Dirac fermions on anisotropic lattices in terms of the three-dimensional Wilson-Dirac Hamiltonian operator. We derive an effective Hamiltonian that governs Euclidean time evolution at finite temporal lattice spacing, and demonstrate its hermiticity and particle-anti-particle symmetry. Our results allow to quantify lattice artifacts of the numerical spectral reconstruction based on Euclidean fermionic correlators at finite temporal lattice spacing.

AI.tldr: The paper develops explicit formulas for the fermionic determinant and propagator of 4D clover-improved Wilson-Dirac fermions on anisotropic lattices, using a 3D Hamiltonian. It also introduces an effective Hamiltonian to describe Euclidean time evolution with finite temporal lattice spacing, which is shown to be hermitian and possess particle-anti-particle symmetry. The findings help in assessing the numerical errors in spectral function reconstruction from Euclidean correlators at finite lattice spacing.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to reconstruct real-time spectral functions from Euclidean correlators within the context of lattice QCD, aiming to better understand and quantify the lattice artifacts that arise when working with finite temporal lattice spacing.
AI.method: The method involves deriving explicit expressions for the fermionic determinant and propagator by relating them to the three-dimensional Wilson-Dirac Hamiltonian operator. An effective Hamiltonian is formulated to govern the Euclidean time evolution, and its properties are demonstrated.
AI.result: The result is the derivation of an effective Hamiltonian that is both hermitian and exhibits particle-anti-particle symmetry, allowing for the quantification of lattice artifacts in the numerical spectral reconstruction process from Euclidean fermionic correlators at finite lattice spacing.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the derived effective Hamiltonian and the explicit expressions for the fermionic determinant and propagator provide a means to assess and potentially reduce the numerical errors associated with spectral function reconstruction in lattice QCD simulations.

title: Confining Flux Tube in the Trace Deformed (2+1) Dimensional SU(2) Gauge Theory
summary: We study the confining flux tube in the reconfined phase of trace deformed SU(2) Yang-Mills theory in (2+1) dimensions. Using lattice simulations above the standard deconfinement temperature, we analyze Polyakov-loop correlators and extract the ground state energy of the effective string. We show that the usual Nambu-Goto effective string description, including its standard higher-order corrections, fails to reproduce the data as the trace deformation is increased. Remarkably, deep in the reconfined regime the results are instead accurately described by the Polchinski-Yang rigid-string solution, corresponding to an effective string dominated by an extrinsic-curvature term. We further investigate the transverse profile of the chromo-electric flux tube and find significant deviations from the standard Yang-Mills behavior, including a substantial modification of the intrinsic width. Finally, we present an exploratory study of the phase diagram, finding evidence for a transition from a continuous to a first order reconfinement line as the deformation parameter increases. These results suggest that the reconfined phase realizes a qualitatively different effective-string regime from ordinary confinement.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the confining flux tube in a reconfined phase of a modified SU(2) Yang-Mills theory. It finds that the Nambu-Goto effective string model does not accurately describe the data at higher trace deformations, while the Polchinski-Yang rigid-string solution fits well. The chromo-electric flux tube's transverse profile shows deviations from standard behavior, and there is evidence for a change in the nature of the reconfinement transition as the deformation parameter increases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the properties of the confining flux tube in the reconfined phase of a trace deformed SU(2) Yang-Mills theory, which may exhibit different characteristics from the ordinary confinement observed in the standard Yang-Mills theory. The authors aim to explore how the effective string description changes with the introduction of trace deformation and to map out the phase diagram of the system.
AI.method: The researchers used lattice simulations to analyze Polyakov-loop correlators and extract the ground state energy of the effective string. They compared their results with theoretical predictions from the Nambu-Goto and Polchinski-Yang models. Additionally, they studied the transverse profile of the chromo-electric flux tube and explored the phase diagram by varying the deformation parameter.
AI.result: The results show that the Nambu-Goto effective string description fails to reproduce the data as the trace deformation is increased, whereas the Polchinski-Yang rigid-string solution provides an accurate description in the reconfined regime. There are significant modifications to the intrinsic width of the chromo-electric flux tube, and evidence suggests a transition from a continuous to a first-order reconfinement line as the deformation parameter increases.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the reconfined phase of the trace deformed SU(2) Yang-Mills theory is characterized by a qualitatively different effective-string regime than ordinary confinement, with the effective string being dominated by an extrinsic-curvature term. The phase diagram exhibits a transition in the nature of the reconfinement line, indicating a rich structure in the theory's phases.
